title: Engineer identifies and explains every '90s computer seen in Jurassic Park
gist: A Google engineer's blog post catalogs the real 1990s hardware used on the Jurassic Park set (SGI workstations, Thinking Machines CM-5s, Macintosh Quadra 700s) worth over $1.7 million combined, illustrating how meticulously tech vendors placed products in the film despite modern Apple's anti-villain product-placement policy.

themes.theme: 1990s tech vendor product placement in film

insights.claim: Jurassic Park's production used real, working computer hardware rather than props, totaling roughly $1.725 million: $875,000 in Silicon Graphics hardware, $350,000 from Apple, and about $500,000 in additional hardware and software, according to a quote from special effects coordinator Cory Faucher shared by Google engineer Fabien Sanglard.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A historical case study in vendor product-placement spend during the tech industry's PC/workstation boom era, useful context for anyone studying brand marketing economics of that period.
insights.quote: All told, $875,000 worth of computer hardware loaned by Silicon Graphics, $350,000 worth from Apple and some $500,000 in additional hardware and software went into equipping both the set and off-stage control room.
insights.locator: Everything in the set was real

insights.claim: Villain character Dennis Nedry's computers in Jurassic Park were Macintosh Quadra 700 machines, which the author notes is ironic given that today's Apple reportedly has a policy against product placement that puts its devices in the hands of villains.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Indicates Apple's brand-control discipline around product placement has tightened materially since the 1990s, a data point for observers of Apple's marketing strategy evolution.
insights.locator: Two of character Dennis Nedry's computers were Macs
